expectations.text: Extracts EVERY scenario from the blueprint as Gherkin given/when/then, including the edge cases (late-joining user, future start date, end-date-before-start-date), both deviation scenarios (concurrent publish wins, dismiss-then-edit keeps dismissal), and the admin-only adversarial scenarios — no corner case is dropped because it is awkward.
Each scenario is tagged with its type (happy_path / edge_case / corner_case) and the plan reflects all three categories rather than only the happy path.

Orders the scenarios explicitly (an order/status table plus per-scenario order) and gives a stated reason for why each scenario sits where it does, justified by reuse / dependency chains (foundational, capability-unlocking scenarios first).

For each scenario, writes a code-blind plan with all four parts: Preconditions, Required capabilities (described as capabilities, e.g. 'a way to persist per-user dismissal', not as existing code), Postconditions, and Risks/assumptions.
expectations.evidence: Each scenario appears to include the four code-blind plan headings.

expectations.text: Writes concrete, answerable research questions per scenario that turn the required capabilities into questions for the research step (specific 'is there an X that does Y, and where?' form), not vague directives like 'look into the banner code'.

expectations.evidence: Planned status appears for scenario sections/table.

expectations.text: NEVER reads, opens, greps, or references source code, and NEVER asserts what already exists in a codebase (no 'this already exists in...', no file paths into a repo); required capabilities are described in the abstract, leaving existence for the research step.
expectations.evidence: No source paths or existing-code claims found.

expectations.text: Treats every scenario as a vertical slice (full stack, end to end) and never orders or structures the plan by horizontal layer (no 'do all the database work first' phase).

eval_feedback.overall: Heuristic inline grader; review qualitative outputs for borderline judgments.
